lines.text: Section three. Natural minimal cells found in the wild.
Let me give you some numbers so that you have a sense of the scale.
Our two workhorse chassis organisms in synthetic biology are E. coli and S. cerevisiae yeast.
E. coli is a prokaryote, about one micrometre in diameter, with a genome of 4.6 million base pairs encoding around 4,500 genes.

S. cerevisiae yeast is a eukaryote — has a nucleus, has organelles — and it is considerably larger, about ten micrometres in diameter, with a genome of 12.5 million base pairs encoding around 5,800 genes.
Despite yeast being ten times the diameter of E. coli and having about three times the DNA, it has only about 1,000 more genes.
That is an interesting clue about how cellular complexity scales: more DNA does not necessarily mean proportionally more genes.
Now, looking for the smallest cells in nature.
Among free-living eukaryotes — those that can survive on their own without being parasites — minimum genome sizes are around 12 million base pairs with about 5,000 genes.
S. cerevisiae sits near this minimum; its close relative S. pombe is a bit smaller.
Some parasitic eukaryotes have considerably smaller genomes, but they have, in essence, outsourced parts of their metabolism to their hosts and so do not count as free-living minimal cells.
A particularly interesting case is Ostreococcus tauri, the smallest known free-living photosynthetic eukaryote.

It is a primitive alga whose cells are as small as E. coli — about one micrometre — and yet each cell contains chloroplasts and starch granules, the kind of organelles you would expect in much larger plant cells.
Its genome is around 12.5 million base pairs with as many as 8,000 genes — physically minimal, but with quite a high gene count.
Among prokaryotes, the smallest cells get much smaller.
Two recently-discovered examples push the gene-count floor remarkably low.
Hodgkinia cicadicola has only 188 genes in a genome of just 144,000 base pairs.
Carsonella ruddii has 213 genes in a similarly tiny genome.
But both Hodgkinia and Carsonella are not free-living — they live as symbionts inside larger insect hosts, and they have shed essential genes that their hosts now provide for them.

Nanoarchaeum equitans, a thermophilic archaeon, is also a symbiont — it cannot live independently — but its cells are only 400 nanometres across and its genome is 490,000 base pairs with about 500 genes.
It is currently the smallest known living organism.
Perhaps the smallest known truly free-living organism is Pelagibacter ubique, a bacterium that thrives in the world's oceans.
Its genome is about 1.3 million base pairs with around 1,300 genes — and its cells are remarkably small, less than a micrometre long and only 200 nanometres wide.
Despite this minimal architecture, Pelagibacter is extraordinarily abundant in the wild — estimates put it at around 50 percent of all cells in temperate oceans during summer.
That is a striking statistic.
The most-studied class of natural minimal cells, though, are the Mycoplasma genus of bacteria.
Mycoplasmas are parasitic, so strictly speaking they are not free-living, but they can be grown independently in the laboratory when given the right nutrients.
They have become the central model organisms for minimal-cell research.
Four species are particularly important.
Mycoplasma pneumoniae, with 816,000 base pairs and 680 genes, has been the focus of extensive systems-biology studies aimed at characterising every aspect of how a minimal cell works.
Mycoplasma mycoides and Mycoplasma capricolum are workhorses for genome-transplantation experiments.
And Mycoplasma genitalium has the smallest genome of any of them — 583,000 base pairs and just 482 genes — making it the central organism in the minimal-genome project we will discuss next.

Across this range of natural cells, what emerges is that the gene-count minimum for free-living organisms — at least in nutrient-rich environments — appears to be somewhere in the range of a few hundred to a couple of thousand genes, depending on the strictness of "free-living".
Below that, organisms exist, but they depend on hosts.
